data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0545 GMT - The physical bullion short-squeeze is poised to send gold above $3,000/oz, says Heng Koon How of UOB's Global Economics & Markets Research in a note. "Trade tariff uncertainties have resulted in a strong and sudden shipment of gold bullion back into COMEX and the U.S., triggering a global short squeeze in gold," says the head of Markets Strategy. Also, current key positive drivers for gold remain intact, such as increasing concerns over a growth slowdown, he adds. UOB recently raised its gold forecasts to $2,900/oz from $2,800/oz for 2Q, $3,000/oz from $2,900/oz for 3Q, and to $3,100/oz from $3,000 for 4Q. Spot gold is little changed at $2,908.57/oz. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2248 GMT - Wednesday could be a good day for Orica investors betting on additional capital management. Barrenjoey believes Orica could announce an on-market share buyback program worth around A$500 million during its investor day, pointing to the headroom to its 30-40% gearing target. Analyst Brook Campbell-Crawford says the investor day could also offer more color on medium-term financial targets for Orica's Digital division. It thinks this would be well received by investors. "We have an overweight rating on Orica given its strong growth outlook, prospect for capital returns, and attractive valuation of 16x near-term EPS (versus its 5-year average of 18x)," Barrenjoey says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2229 GMT - The risk of new delays to Deep Yellow's Tumas project helps to keep Jefferies at underperform on the aspiring uranium producer. Deep Yellow aims to make a final investment decision on Tumas this month, while arranging a debt package and finding offtake partners. Current pre-production capex is estimated at US$360 million, plus US$51 million in pre-production capitalized operating costs, and US$30 million in working capital. "We see risk of elevated capex and project delays to the 2026 first production timeline," says analyst Daniel Roden. "Deep Yellow has also stated the development of Tumas will only be pursued with an unquantified supportive uranium price to provide the best shareholder returns." (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2211 GMT - Uranium prices may have fallen 12% so far this year but Jefferies believes the medium-term outlook for the nuclear fuel remains bright. It attributes recent weakness in uranium prices to heightened geopolitical and macro uncertainties, cautious utility buying, and increased confidence in supply. "The risk-off environment has largely kept buyers out of the spot market, while U.S. utilities continue to engage in the term market, with 11 term awards totalling 15.3 million lbs already secured in 2025," analyst Daniel Roden says. That has driven a widening contango between term prices of uranium at US$80.00/lb and spot prices of US$63.95/lb. Still, Jefferies stays constructive on the medium-term outlook, supported by disciplined production strategies, supply deficits, and rising global reactor demand from China, U.S. and Europe. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0754 GMT - German arms makers Rheinmetall, Hensoldt and Renk Group will benefit from the decision agreed by Germany's likely coalition partners to allow defense spending beyond 1% of gross domestic product to be exempted from Germany's current deficit limit, Berenberg analysts say. The center-right CDU, its CSU sister party and the Social Democratic Party also agreed to create a new 500 billion-euro special fund for infrastructure. Berenberg estimates that the German defense budget will increase to 2.6% of GDP by 2028, reaching 111 billion euros by comparison with 52 billion euros in 2024. Hensoldt derives 56% of its revenue from Germany, above the 27% of Renk. In the case of Rheinmetall, 19% of revenue comes from Germany in its defense business. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - European military budgets will grow at least at a high-single-digit until 2035, in parallel with a push by regional governments to order European defense equipment over U.S. kit, boosting European defense stocks, Berenberg analysts say. They note that Britain's commitment to raise U.K. defense spending to 2.5% of its gross domestic product by 2027 was higher than they anticipated, and will benefit QinetiQ and Babcock International in particular, which get more than half of their revenue from the U.K. Ministry of Defence. Berenberg upgraded Babcock International to buy from hold, saying consensus growth estimates are too low, and downgraded BAE Systems and Chemring to hold from buy due to defense budget uncertainty in the U.S., which provides the highest share of their revenue. Its top pick is German arms maker Rheinmetall. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0257 GMT - Singapore Post is likely to declare special dividends along with its full-year results in May, says Maybank Securities analyst Jarick Seet in a note. After the postal and e-commerce logistics provider pares its Australian debt and the potential sale of its subsidiary, Famous Holdings, Seet expects around S$400 million-S$450 million in excess proceeds from the sale. This could be distributed as special dividends to shareholders, Seet says. As end-2024, SingPost held about S$391.5 million in cash and Seet reckons that the company will not need to keep cash from the sales proceeds on its balance sheet. Maybank maintains the stock's buy rating and target price of S$0.77. Shares are at S$0.57.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0130 GMT - Bumi Armada's recent three-year contract with India's Oil & Natural Gas Corp. could provide the Malaysian company with a stable income stream, replacing the previous arrangement of an annual renewal, CIMB Securities analyst Nurzulaikha Azali says in a note. The contract, worth 578.4 million ringgit, could contribute approximately 56.7 million ringgit annually to Bumi Armada's profit, she says. The analyst raises her 2025-2027 earnings estimates on Bumi Armada by 7.1%, 10% and 9.4%, respectively, to reflect the new contract. CIMB Securities raises Bumi Armada's target price to MYR0.71 from MYR0.69 while maintaining a buy rating on the stock. Shares are unchanged at MYR0.54. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: China Environmental Energy Investment Proposes Name Change
data.description:
China Environmental Energy Investment proposed changing its name to "Dufu Liquor Group Limited" to align with its shift from jewelry to liquor sales, according to a Monday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The decision will be voted on at a special general meeting on April 1. If approved, the new name will take effect after regulatory approval in Bermuda.

The company recently signed an agreement to distribute Sichuan Dufujiu's liquor products across Asia.

The company's share price was up by 6% in recent trading.

data.title: India's Infosys rises after CLSA upgrades to 'outperform'
data.description:
** Shares of India's No.2 IT company Infosys rise 1.4% to 1,710 rupees; stock top pct gainer on Nifty IT index

** CLSA upgrades stock to "outperform" from "hold"; PT at 1,978 rupees

** Discretionary demand revival across key verticals and geographies intact, supporting continued growth for the IT services major - CLSA

** Adds, current valuation levels present attractive investment opportunity

** INFY's demand pipeline remains strong, especially in high-growth sectors such as cloud computing, data analytics, and enterprise resource planning - CLSA

** Analysts covering INFY on avg rate it "buy", while rivals HCLTech and Wipro rated "hold" on avg, per data compiled by LSEG

** INFY has shed 10% so far this year vs a 12.6% fall in NIFTYIT

data.title: Week Ahead for FX, Bonds: U.S. Inflation Data, Bank of Canada Decision Due
data.description:
U.S. inflation data for February will be the highlight among economic data as investors continue to follow the back-and-forth of U.S. President Trump's announcements on tariffs.

A rate decision by the Bank of Canada is due, where rates are likely to be cut amid concern about the economic impact of U.S. tariffs on Canadian imports.

In Asia, the focus is on China's annual legislature session, which will end during the week, as well as a slew of data releases from Japan.

U.S.

As more headlines from President Trump on tariffs and Ukraine will continue to be closely monitored, Wednesday's U.S. inflation data for February will be the highlight of the week's economic data.

The U.S. Federal Reserve has raised concerns recently that inflation isn't slowing as much as it had hoped. The data could continue to reflect this, particularly given evidence that the prospect of tariffs has already been feeding through into higher prices.

Business surveys suggest that some companies are pre-empting tariffs by lifting prices, ING economists said in a note. Food prices have also been rising, while energy prices could additionally contribute to more inflation, they said.

Still, the impact of the data on U.S. rate expectations could be limited because of growing concerns about the impact of Trump's policies, especially tariffs, on the economy.

"The market appears more focused on worries about growth right now in an environment of government austerity and the threat of a squeeze on spending power if prices do respond to tariffs," ING said.

U.S. money markets are now pricing in three 25 basis-point rate cuts from the U.S. Federal Reserve this year, versus just one only a few weeks ago.

"A 0.3% month-on-month inflation print is unlikely to change that situation."

With doubts starting to grow about the strength of the U.S. economy, the University of Michigan March preliminary consumer survey on Friday, March 14 could also be significant for gauging sentiment amid tariff uncertainty and public-sector job losses.

Other data include February producer price data and weekly jobless claims figures on Thursday.

On tariffs, the U.S. is set to impose steel and aluminium tariffs of 25% on the EU from Wednesday.

The Treasury will auction $58 billion in three-year notes on Tuesday, $39 billion in 10-year notes on Wednesday and $22 billion in 30-year bonds on Thursday.

CANADA

Canada is one of U.S. President Trump's key targets for tariffs, putting Canadian data and events more in the spotlight than usual.

The Bank of Canada announces a rate decision on Wednesday. With Canada's jobs data for February coming in weak, even before tariffs were implemented, a rate cut looks increasingly likely.

"The slowdown in hiring even before tariffs were implemented gives the BOC an easy pass to cut interest rates another quarter point," economists at Desjardins Group said in a note.

Money markets are pricing in a 78% chance of a rate cut at the upcoming meeting, LSEG data show.

EUROZONE

The data calendar in the coming week is slim, leaving investors continuing to ponder the implications of Germany's large-scale fiscal stimulus plans--which caused a steep rise in German government-bond yields--as well as a planned step-up in defense spending on the continent.

Investors are particularly interested in how the fiscal expansion will impact Europe's growth and inflation, and how that could impact the European Central Bank's monetary policy.

Vanguard and AllianceBernstein already expect fewer ECB rate cuts after this month's 25-basis-point reduction in the deposit rate to 2.50%. Vanguard now expects only one more rate cut this year, while AllianceBernstein's revised baseline for the ECB's policy rate at the end of 2025 is 2%, up from a previous expectation of 1.75%.

Industrial production data from Germany for January are due Monday, followed by eurozone industrial production figures Thursday. Final February inflation data from France and Spain are due on Friday, March 14.

Germany will auction 4.5 billion euros in March 2027 Schatz on Tuesday and 4.5 billion euros in February 2035 Bund on Wednesday. Portugal holds an auction on Wednesday and Italy on Thursday.

U.K.

U.K. gross domestic product data for January are due Friday, March 14 and come after unexpectedly strong GDP for December.

Investec economist Philip Shaw said the data could continue to be solid, but there are many uncertainties ahead which could weigh on growth going forward.

"We are forecasting a 0.2% increase in GDP on the month, but would emphasise that this represents a brisker pace of expansion than is likely over the remainder of the year," he said.

The U.K. plans to launch a new September 2049 index-linked gilt via syndication in the week starting on March 10, subject to demand and market conditions. The U.K. plans the sale of March 2035 gilt Wednesday.

POLAND

Poland's central bank announces a rate decision on Wednesday. It is expected to leave its key interest rate on hold at 5.75% due to continued concerns about inflation.

SCANDINAVIA, SWITZERLAND

Sweden will hold a bond auction on Wednesday. Switzerland will hold a bond auction on Wednesday.

CHINA

China's annual legislature session will end Tuesday when lawmakers are expected to approve a government work report that includes an ambitious 2025 growth target of around 5% for the world's second-largest economy.

Attention is on whether Beijing will follow up on stimulus pledges with more concrete measures, with senior officials having announced plans to step up fiscal spending and boost consumption.

Next week, China's central bank will likely release its gauges of the country's credit growth for February. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal estimated that banks issued 1.23 trillion yuan of new loans last month, a significant fall from January's 5.13 trillion yuan, mainly due to seasonality. Meanwhile, M2, a broad gauge of money supply, may have expanded 7% in February from a year earlier, matching the pace seen in January.

JAPAN

The Japanese Trade Union Confederation, known as Rengo, is scheduled to announce preliminary results for annual spring wage negotiations on Friday, March 14. Japan's largest trade union group has said its member unions are seeking an average wage increase of 6.09%, the biggest request in three decades. These wage negotiations are closely watched by economists and investors for clues on the timing of future Bank of Japan interest-rate increases.

Revised gross domestic product data, scheduled to be released Tuesday, is likely to confirm that the Japanese economy expanded for the third-straight quarter during the October-December period. The preliminary report released in February showed that real GDP grew an annualized 2.8% in the final quarter of 2024.

Separate government data due Tuesday may also signal a recovery in private consumption. Household spending is expected to have increased 3.5% in January from a year earlier, according to a poll of economists by data provider Quick. That compares with the 2.7% rise seen in December.

Figures on current account balance for January and bank lending for February are also slated for release Monday.

The Ministry of Finance is scheduled to auction 2.3 trillion yen of five-year sovereign notes on Monday and 1 trillion yen of 20-year JGBs on Wednesday. The 20-year JGBs to be auctioned will be a reopening of the January 2025 issue, the ministry said. Investors such as insurance companies and pension funds are likely to show more bidding interest at the 20-year auction owing to the issue's higher yields that will probably be available.

AUSTRALIA

On Tuesday, National Australia Bank's business survey for February will attract some attention as markets weigh whether global events might be starting to dent confidence.

Australia's economy has been supported by government spending over the last year, with private demand subdued. The NAB survey may offer some clues on a private demand recovery, which will help to offset an expected slide in public demand later this year.

INDIA

India's February inflation print on Wednesday will likely show a further cooling in price pressures, which could pave the way for further monetary policy easing.

Bank of America economist Rahul Bajoria forecasts headline inflation to have eased to 4.0%, the midpoint of the central bank's target range, which would mark the slowest price growth in six months. Food and vegetable prices likely eased during the month, Bajoria said.

The Reserve Bank of India sees food inflation pressures softening in the coming months, but has warned that continued volatility in energy prices and adverse weather conditions pose upside risks to prices.

Given recent data showing a slowdown in growth and easing inflation, ANZ's Dhiraj Nim and Sanjay Mathur see a "case for a stronger policy response" by the central bank. The economists said in a note that the RBI could do more to stimulate the economy, and expect the RBI to deliver at least two more rate cuts by June. ANZ pegs India's inflation at 3.8% for February.

  • Any references to days are in local times.

Write to Jessica Fleetham at jessica.fleetham@wsj.com and Hoi Shan Chan at hoishan.chan@wsj.com

data.title: Thai Central Bank More Likely to Cut Rates in August Than in April — Market Talk
data.description: There may be some hurdles for the Bank of Thailand to deliver a consecutive rate cut in April, Citi Research economists say in a note. The central bank had already factored in some of the risks associated with additional U.S. tariff hikes when delivering its surprise rate cut in February, they write. Citi lowers its 2025 and 2026 inflation forecasts to 0.9% and 1.0%, respectively, from 1.0% and 1.4%. Its 2025 inflation forecast is below the BOT's, reinforcing the risk of inflation undershooting the central bank's target for a second consecutive year. The pressure for another rate cut will persist, particularly as falling credit growth alleviates concerns about the financial stability costs of monetary easing, they say. Citi reiterates its expectation for another 25 bps cut in August.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Checkpoint Therapeutics to Be Acquired by India's Sun Pharmaceutical
data.description:
Checkpoint Therapeutics has agreed to be acquired by India's Sun Pharmaceutical Industries for $4.10 per share in cash plus a non-transferable contingent value right, the companies said Sunday.

The contingent value right entitles the stockholder to receive up to an additional $0.70 in cash if metastatic cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma treatment cosibelimab is approved before deadlines in the European Union or in certain countries, the companies said.

Checkpoint, its controlling shareholder Fortress Biotech , and Sun Pharma have also entered into a royalty agreement under which Fortress will receive royalties from future sales of cosibelimab during a specified term.

The proposed transaction is expected to close in Q2, the companies said.

data.title: Austria Industrial Output Rebounds
data.description:
Industrial production in Austria rose by 0.5% year-on-year in January 2025, rebounding from a revised 4.7% decline in the previous month.

Production for consumer non-durables rose significantly (10.6% vs 4% in December), while that for intermediate goods rebounded (1.8% vs -5.6%).

However, output for energy (-9.6% vs -9.2%) and consumer durables (-15.3% vs -13.8%) dropped further.

Output for capital goods also decreased, albeit at a slower pace (-2.3% vs -6.8%).

On a seasonally adjusted monthly basis, industrial output increased by 3% in January, accelerating from a revised 0.8% rise in December.

data.title: Wise Ally International's Profit to Swell in 2024
data.description:
Wise Ally International expects an attributable profit of between HK$60 million and HK$80 million for the year 2024, up from HK$1.2 million a year prior, a March 7 filing with the Hong Kong bourse said.

The electronics manufacturer attributed the higher anticipated profit mainly to a rise in revenue and gross profit margin, as well as the absence of a loss on fair value changes of financial assets at fair value through profit or loss.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Monday at 4 AM ET
data.description:
Crude prices end higher on report of U.S. plan to refill its oil reserve

Oil futures settled higher Friday, buoyed by reported U.S. plans to refill the nation's Strategic Petroleum Reserve to full capacity, which would lead to tighter global supplies.

Nearly Half of Energy Executives Think We Won't Reach Net Zero Until 2070

Rising capital project costs and a lack of optimism around ESG are pushing back environmental goals, according to a survey of industry leaders from across oil and gas, utilities, chemicals, mining, and agribusiness.

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on tariffs aimed at Canada, the U.S. rig count, Petrobras, and more in the latest Market Talks covering energy and utilities

Harbour Energy Shares Sink to Record Low After Profit Hit by U.K. Windfall Tax

The oil-and-gas producer swung to a loss in 2024 as the energy profits levy continued to drag on results and its CEO described the U.K. as a difficult fiscal and regulatory environment in which to operate.

Sunnova Bondholders Engage Advisers Following KKR Deal, Going-Concern Warning

A group of bondholders in Sunnova Energy International has organized and hired legal counsel after the rooftop solar panel company issued a going concern notice and said it raised financing from KKR.

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Rise More Than Expected

U.S. crude oil inventories rose by 3.6 million barrels last week, which was more than expected, resuming an upward trend after a surprise drawdown the week before.

DTEK in Talks With U.S. Suppliers to Boost Gas Supply to Europe

Ukraine's largest private energy company is in discussions with U.S. suppliers to bring more natural gas to Europe as part of a broader expansion, its CEO said in an interview.

Nuclear Power's Revival Is Here. What Do You Do With All the Radioactive Waste?

Tons of radioactive refuse go into temporary storage every year, and new efforts to store nuclear waste away from reactor sites are getting pushback.

Oil Outlook Clouded by OPEC+ Output, Trade Tariff Risks

Higher-than-expected supply and a potential demand hit due to softer U.S. activity and escalating tariffs pose downside risks to its oil price forecast, said Goldman Sachs.

U.S. Refiners Import Lots of Oil. Trump's Tariffs Spell Trouble.

Supplies from Canada and Mexico are at the heart of the U.S. oil industry.

data.title: India's Consumer Inflation Likely Eased in February, WSJ Poll Shows — Market Talk
data.description: India's consumer inflation is expected to have eased to 3.8% on year in February, according to the median estimate of 11 economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. That would compare with the 4.3% rise in January's CPI. Economists mostly expect broad-based declines in food and vegetable prices to drag inflation lower. Inflation is unlikely to rebound, giving the central bank room to ease rates to support the economy, Capital Economics' Shilan Shah and Joe Maher write in a note. "Weak domestic demand, higher crop output and muted manufacturing costs are likely to offset upside risks from a weak currency," Nomura research analysts say in a note. Both expect an additional 75 bps of rate cuts this year, which would bring the repo rate to 5.5% by the year's end. The inflation data are due Wednesday. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

data.title: Tecnicas Reunidas To Carry Out Engineering Services To Develop Green Methanol Plant In Spain
data.description:
Tecnicas Reunidas SA :

  • PROJECT IN CONSORTIUM WITH SIEMENS ENERGY

  • FACILITY TO HAVE A PRODUCTION CAPACITY OF 140,000 TONS PER YEAR

data.title: BioVersys to Issue Additional Shares After IPO Over-allotment
data.description:
BioVersys said Monday it would issue additional shares following the partial exercise of the over-allotment option related to its initial public offering on the SIX Swiss Exchange.

As a result, the Swiss biopharmaceutical company will issue an additional 47,862 new shares, bringing the total number of new shares issued in connection with the IPO to 2,131,195 and outstanding shares to 5,823,480.

The company raised 76.7 million francs in gross proceeds through the IPO, based on the offer price of 36 francs per share.

data.title: Baazar Style Retail Opens New Store in Assam, India; Shares Up 4%
data.description:
Baazar Style Retail has opened a new store of Style Baazar at Golaghat in Assam, India on Monday, according to a same-day filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

With this, the company's total number of stores has increased to 210.

Shares of the company were up nearly 4% in recent trade.

data.title: Julphar to Halt Trading on Abu Dhabi Bourse
data.description:
Gulf Pharmaceutical Industries (ADX:JULPHAR), d/b/a Julphar, will temporarily pause trading on the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange from 11 am local time on March 17 due to its board meeting.

The Emirati pharmaceutical company will resume trading once its meeting results are disclosed, according to a Monday release.

data.title: Vinanz to Evaluate Dual Listing on Nasdaq
data.description:
Vinanz tapped boutique securities and corporate finance law firm Lucosky Brookman to evaluate the possibility of a dual listing on Nasdaq, according to a Monday release.

The discussions are at an early stage, with no certainty that the listing will take place, the London-listed Bitcoin mining company's Chairman David Lenigas added.

data.title: B.R.Goyal Infrastructure Bags New Order Worth INR113 Million
data.description:
B.R.Goyal Infrastructure has received a work order worth 112.8 million Indian rupees from Bhavnagar Municipal Corporation in Guajrat, India, according to a Monday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The contract is for the construction of a four-lane road from Akwada Ward to Ruva Ravechidham Ring Road at Bhavnagar in Gujarat.

The project is expected to be completed in 10 months.
